question: Is the NuScale approach of eliminating emergency diesel generators (Ch.01) adequately justified by the passive safety systems in Ch.05?

nuscale_ch01_0413.title: SRP 9.5.7, Rev 3: Emergency Diesel Engine Lubrication SystemAll (II.1 thru II.4) Compliance with GDC 2, GDC 4, GDC 5, and GDC 17
nuscale_ch01_0413.summary: The NuScale plant design does not require or include safety-related emergency diesel generators, and therefore the associated systems (fuel oil storage, cooling water, starting, lubrication, and air intake/exhaust) and their compliance with GDC 2, 4, 5, and 17 are not applicable. The plant does not rely on AC or DC power for the performance of its safety functions.

nuscale_ch01_0812.title: DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0: Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review ResponsibilitiesII.6 Backup options if licensing solutions declared inoperable
nuscale_ch01_0812.summary: The NuScale design does not use exclusion zone or detect-and-suppress methods for thermal hydraulic stability, as reactor trip signals act before unstable flow oscillations can develop. Backup options are not required because existing technical specifications for the Reactor Trip System (RTS) already control allowable unavailabilities of protective trips, ensuring safety without additional measures.

nuscale_ch01_0050.title: Diesel Generator Buildings
nuscale_ch01_0050.summary: The Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs) in the NuScale Power Plant each house a single backup diesel generator and their auxiliary equipment, but do not contain any safety-related systems or support engineered safety functions (ESFs). Their primary purpose is to provide support and housing for the generators, associated equipment, and maintenance areas.

nuscale_ch01_0022.title: Engineered Safety Feature Materials
nuscale_ch01_0022.summary: The NuScale reactor containment vessel (CNV) is maintained under vacuum during normal operations, which reduces corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits combustible gases. The design uses passive safety features—such as natural heat removal and elimination of large external coolant loops—which improve safety, reduce core damage frequency, and minimize radioactive source term compared to conventional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0020.title: Safety Considerations
nuscale_ch01_0020.summary: The NuScale design enhances safety by maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV), which reduces corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits combustible gases. Its passive safety systems and integral design eliminate the need for external power and large coolant loops, significantly lowering the risk of core damage and making the plant safer than conventional light water reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0412.title: SRP 9.5.6, Rev 3: Emergency Diesel Engine Starting SystemAll (II.1 thru II.4)Compliance with GDC 2, GDC 4, GDC 5, and GDC 17
nuscale_ch01_0412.summary: The NuScale plant design does not include or require safety-related emergency diesel generators, and therefore the associated systems (fuel oil storage, cooling water, starting, lubrication, and air intake/exhaust) and their compliance with regulatory criteria are not applicable. The plant’s safety functions do not rely on either AC or DC power sources.

nuscale_ch01_0669.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.4 Specific CriterionInstrument Spans and Setpoints use RG 1.105
nuscale_ch01_0669.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for abnormal operating occurrences such as changes in feedwater temperature, flow, steam flow, and inadvertent system operations. It confirms that NuScale meets requirements for system pressure, maintains the minimum critical heat flux ratio (MCHFR) above safety limits, ensures that anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs) do not escalate to more serious conditions, and uses Regulatory Guide 1.105 for instrument spans and setpoints. Overall, NuScale’s design aligns with the relevant sections of the Standard Review Plan and Design Specific Review Standard.

nuscale_ch01_0044.title: Chemical and Volume Control System
nuscale_ch01_0044.summary: The Chemical and Volume Control System section describes the NuScale reactor’s closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation cooling system, which uses two independent trains of decay heat removal equipment, each capable of fully cooling the reactor. In the event of a steam generator tube failure or loss of AC power, passive systems transfer decay heat to a large, stainless steel-lined pool (the ultimate heat sink), ensuring continued cooling through natural circulation and heat rejection to the atmosphere or external sinks.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain fuel integrity, minimize solid deposits, and ensure uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0024.title: Emergency Core Cooling System
nuscale_ch01_0024.summary: The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) in the NuScale design is a fully passive system that removes decay heat during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) using three independent reactor vent valves and two recirculation valves, all of which are closed during normal operation. During ECCS activation, steam is vented and condensed in the containment vessel, with recirculation valves allowing water to cool the core, and heat is ultimately transferred passively to the reactor pool.

nuscale_ch01_0063.title: Emergency Core Cooling System Valve Design Certification Application Demonstration Test
nuscale_ch01_0063.summary: This section describes the demonstration test program for the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) valves in the NuScale reactor design. The program aimed to verify the functional performance of the ECCS valves, including their operation at reactor pressures and temperatures and in borated coolant, as well as the effectiveness of unique design features like the inadvertent actuation block (IAB). Testing was conducted at a specialized facility using a test article representative of the reactor recirculation valve (RRV) to ensure the valves meet safety and operational requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0666.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.1 Specific CriterionSystem Pressure
nuscale_ch01_0666.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for abnormal operational events such as changes in feedwater temperature, flow, steam flow, and inadvertent system operations. It confirms that NuScale meets requirements for system pressure, critical heat flux limits, event classification, and instrument setpoints, ensuring plant safety and compliance with the Standard Review Plan and Design Specific Review Standard. No exceptions or non-conformances are noted.

nuscale_ch01_0202.title: DSRS 7.1.5, Rev 0: Diversity and Defense in DepthII.1 Methods for performing D3 analyses of reactor protection systems
nuscale_ch01_0202.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for reactor protection systems, specifically focusing on diversity and defense-in-depth (D3) analyses. It details compliance with various regulatory guides and standards, including NUREG/CR-6303, SECY-93-087, and 10 CFR 50.62, confirming that the NuScale I&C design meets the required guidelines for safety, independence, redundancy, and quality assurance.

nuscale_ch01_0209.title: DSRS 7.2.1 Rev. 0: Quality II.2 Conformance to RG 1.152
nuscale_ch01_0209.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various regulatory guides and standards for safety-related instrumentation and control (I&C) systems. Specifically, DSRS 7.2.1 Rev. 0: Quality II.2 confirms that the NuScale design meets the requirements of Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.152, as referenced in Table 1.9-2. The document provides a cross-reference to where detailed compliance information can be found.

nuscale_ch01_0278.title: SRP Appendix 8-A, Rev1: General Agenda, Station Site VisitsAll Various
nuscale_ch01_0278.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various regulatory criteria related to DC power systems and station blackout requirements. It notes that NuScale partially conforms to several regulatory guides, with some requirements not applicable due to the design’s passive safety features and the responsibilities assigned to the COL applicant. Additionally, SRP Appendix 8-A is identified as not applicable, as it pertains to NRC staff site visits during licensing reviews rather than design certification.

nuscale_ch01_0275.title: DSRS 8.4, Rev 0: Station BlackoutII.1 Compliance with 10 CFR 50.63 and the guidelines of RG 1.155
nuscale_ch01_0275.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s compliance with regulatory requirements for station blackout (SBO) and related DC power systems. The design partially conforms to several regulatory guides (RG 1.155, RG 1.153, RG 1.63) and 10 CFR 50.63, with some requirements not applicable due to the passive safety features of the NuScale design, which can maintain safety functions for 72 hours without AC power. Responsibilities for certain maintenance and site-specific requirements are assigned to the COL applicant referencing the certified design.

nuscale_ch01_0712.title: DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, Rev 0: Loss of External Load; Turbine Trip; Loss of Condenser Vacuum; Closure of Main Steam Isolation Valve; and Steam Pressure Regulator Failure (Closed)II.3.A Values of Parameters Used in Analytical Model - Initial Power Level and Modes of Operation
nuscale_ch01_0712.summary: This section confirms that the analytical models used for evaluating events such as loss of external load, turbine trip, loss of condenser vacuum, main steam isolation valve closure, and steam pressure regulator failure (closed) conform to the regulatory criteria specified in DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5. It specifically states that the values of parameters used in the models—such as initial power level, modes of operation, scram characteristics, core burnup, and instrumentation setpoints—are in compliance, with no exceptions noted. The referenced table indicates ongoing conformance with the applicable sections of the Standard Review Plan and Design Specific Review Standard.

nuscale_ch01_0715.title: DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, Rev 0: Loss of External Load; Turbine Trip; Loss of Condenser Vacuum; Closure of Main Steam Isolation Valve; and Steam Pressure Regulator Failure (Closed)II.3.D Values of Parameters Used in Analytical Model - Instrumentation Setpoints for Mitigating System Actuation
nuscale_ch01_0715.summary: This section confirms that the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report meets the regulatory criteria for DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5 events, including loss of external load, turbine trip, loss of condenser vacuum, main steam isolation valve closure, and steam pressure regulator failure (closed). Specifically, it states that the analytical model's parameters—such as instrumentation setpoints for mitigating system actuation—conform to requirements, with no exceptions noted.

edges.description: Section A explains that the NuScale design eliminates the need for emergency diesel generators by not relying on AC or DC power for safety functions, while Section B describes how passive safety features (e.g., reactor trip signals) provide protective actions without backup power, thus supporting the justification for eliminating emergency diesel generators.

Section A states that the NuScale design does not require or include safety-related emergency diesel generators due to reliance on passive safety systems, while Section B describes the Central Utility Building as nonsafety-related and does not mention housing emergency diesel generators, supporting the claim in Section A.

Section A states that NuScale's design eliminates the need for emergency diesel generators by not relying on AC or DC power for safety functions, while Section B describes passive safety features (such as maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel) that support this design choice.

Section A states that NuScale does not require emergency diesel generators due to reliance on passive safety systems, while Section B describes specific passive safety features (e.g., vacuum in the CNV) that contribute to plant safety, thus providing technical justification for the design choice in Section A.

Section A and Section B both state that the NuScale plant design does not require or include safety-related emergency diesel generators, referencing the same regulatory criteria and justifying non-applicability based on the plant's reliance on passive safety systems rather than AC or DC power.

Section A discusses the NuScale design's reliance on passive safety systems and reactor trip signals instead of traditional backup options like emergency diesel generators, while Section B describes the diesel generator buildings as nonsafety-related, indicating that the design intentionally does not depend on them for safety functions.

Section A discusses backup safety options and the exclusion of certain methods in the NuScale design, while Section B describes passive safety features (vacuum in the CNV) that contribute to system reliability, thus Section B provides supporting evidence for the adequacy of passive safety systems referenced in Section A.

Section B describes passive safety features of the NuScale design (such as maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel) that support the justification in Section A for not requiring traditional backup options like emergency diesel generators.

Section A discusses NuScale's reliance on passive safety systems and reactor trip signals to prevent thermal hydraulic instability, while Section B states that the NuScale design does not require emergency diesel generators because no AC or DC power is needed for safety functions, thus both sections provide technical and regulatory justification for eliminating emergency diesel generators based on passive safety features.

Section A describes the Central Utility Building's functions, which do not include safety-related emergency diesel generators, while Section B outlines passive safety features (such as the containment vessel vacuum) that may serve as justification for not requiring diesel generators, connecting them through the adequacy of passive safety as a replacement for active backup power.

Section A describes the physical infrastructure related to utility services, including the absence of safety-related emergency diesel generators, while Section B explicitly states that the NuScale design does not require or include such generators because safety functions do not rely on AC or DC power, thus Section B provides the regulatory justification for the design approach described in Section A.

Section A and Section B are identical excerpts describing the benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the CNV, thus they reinforce each other's claims about safety feature materials and safety considerations.

Section B states that the NuScale design does not require emergency diesel generators because no AC or DC power is relied upon for safety functions, while Section A describes features of the containment vessel that support passive safety, thus Section A provides evidence supporting the justification in Section B.

Section A describes passive safety features of the NuScale design that reduce reliance on active systems, while Section B states that emergency diesel generators are not required because no AC or DC power is needed for safety functions, thus Section A's design features are used to justify the elimination described in Section B.

Section A discusses the NuScale plant's conformance with regulatory criteria for certain operational transients, while Section B explicitly states that the NuScale design does not require emergency diesel generators due to reliance on passive safety systems, thus Section B provides justification for the design approach referenced in Section A.

Section A describes how NuScale's system pressure and instrument setpoints conform to regulatory criteria for certain transients, while Section B explains that NuScale's design uses reactor trip signals (rather than backup options like exclusion zones) to prevent thermal hydraulic instability, both addressing how passive and automatic systems replace traditional active safety features like emergency diesel generators.

Section A discusses regulatory criteria and conformance for system responses to certain operational events, while Section B describes the physical plant structures and notes that the Central Utility Building (where diesel generators would typically be located) is nonsafety-related, connecting them through the context of whether safety functions (like emergency power) are required or justified by passive safety systems.

Section A describes the passive safety systems (decay heat removal via natural circulation and passive condensers) that eliminate the need for emergency diesel generators, while Section B documents that the NuScale design does not include or require emergency diesel generators because no AC or DC power is relied upon for safety functions, thus Section A's design approach is presented as the justification for the regulatory position in Section B.

Section A describes the passive decay heat removal capabilities of the NuScale system, which are intended to fulfill the backup safety functions that Section B requires as alternatives to traditional active systems like emergency diesel generators.

Section A describes the passive safety systems (decay heat removal) that are intended to replace the emergency diesel generators referenced in Section B, thus providing the technical justification for their elimination.

Section A describes the passive safety features of the NuScale reactor coolant system that rely on natural circulation and do not require active components, while Section B states that emergency diesel generators are not included or required because no AC or DC power is needed for safety functions, thus Section A's design approach is used to justify the regulatory position in Section B.

Section A describes the NuScale reactor's reliance on passive natural circulation for coolant flow, while Section B outlines regulatory review criteria for backup safety options, connecting them through the need to justify the elimination of active safety systems (like emergency diesel generators) by demonstrating that passive systems meet regulatory safety requirements.

Section A describes the design basis for the reactor coolant system relying on passive natural circulation without active pumps, while Section B notes that the Central Utility Building, which would typically house emergency diesel generators, is nonsafety-related and does not mention such generators, connecting the justification for eliminating emergency diesel generators to the reliance on passive safety systems.

Section A describes the passive ECCS design that removes decay heat without AC or DC power, while Section B states that emergency diesel generators are not required because no safety functions rely on such power, thus Section A's passive system is presented as the justification for the elimination referenced in Section B.

Section A describes the passive Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) as NuScale's method for decay heat removal during a LOCA, while Section B outlines regulatory review criteria for backup safety options, indicating that NuScale relies on passive systems and reactor trip signals instead of traditional backup options like emergency diesel generators.

Section A describes the passive Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) that provides decay heat removal without reliance on active systems like diesel generators, while Section B notes that diesel generator buildings are not safety-related, connecting them through the justification that passive systems in A replace the safety function traditionally provided by B.

Section A provides test evidence of the operability and effectiveness of passive safety system components (ECCS valves), which is used to justify the claim in Section B that emergency diesel generators are not required for NuScale's safety functions.

Section A provides test results confirming the operability of ECCS valves, which are part of the passive safety systems that serve as justification for eliminating emergency diesel generators as discussed in Section B's regulatory review context.

Section B provides test results confirming the operability and performance of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) valves, which supports the justification in Section A that passive safety systems (such as ECCS) can adequately address certain accident scenarios, thereby supporting the elimination of emergency diesel generators as discussed in the regulatory conformance context.
Section A provides detailed instrument spans and setpoints (using RG 1.105) that specify how the system pressure criteria in Section B are monitored and controlled for the same set of transients.

Section A describes the passive decay heat removal capability of the NuScale system, while Section B states that the system conforms to regulatory criteria for specific transients, indicating that the passive system in A satisfies the requirements in B.

Section A provides test results demonstrating the operability of ECCS valves, which are part of the passive safety systems that are relevant to justifying the elimination of emergency diesel generators as discussed in Section B's regulatory conformance criteria.

Section A outlines the conformance of NuScale's instrumentation and control systems with regulatory criteria for safety, while Section B explicitly states that the NuScale design does not require emergency diesel generators because no AC or DC power is relied upon for safety functions, thus connecting the justification for eliminating EDGs (Section B) to the safety system design principles and regulatory compliance described in Section A.

Section A outlines the regulatory criteria and conformance for instrumentation and control (I&C) systems important to safety, while Section B addresses backup options and protective actions for thermal hydraulic stability, connecting them through the requirement that passive safety systems and I&C must collectively ensure safety in the absence of emergency diesel generators.

Section A outlines NuScale's conformance with regulatory criteria for safety-related I&C systems, while Section B discusses backup safety options and the exclusion of certain thermal hydraulic stability methods, both addressing how the design meets or substitutes for traditional safety requirements such as emergency diesel generators.
